sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A galvanic cell was assembled from Zn(s)|Zn2+(aq) and Cu2+(aq)|Cu(s) half-cells at 25°C. The standard reduction potentials are E°(Cu2+/Cu) = +0.34 V and E°(Zn2+/Zn) = -0.76 V. Under standard conditions the cell voltage is 1.10 V. In a nonstandard trial, [Zn2+] was 0.010 M and [Cu2+] was 1.0 M. The reaction quotient for Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) -> Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s) is Q = [Zn2+]/[Cu2+].
questions.stem: Which metal is oxidized in the galvanic cell?

explanations.B: Correct. Zinc has the lower reduction potential and serves as the anode in the spontaneous galvanic cell. It is oxidized according to Zn(s) -> Zn2+(aq) + 2e-, while Cu2+ is reduced at the cathode.
explanations.A: Copper metal is produced at the cathode; it is not the species oxidized in this cell.
explanations.C: Cu2+ gains electrons and is reduced, not oxidized.
explanations.D: Zn2+ is the product of oxidation rather than the reactant being oxidized.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Cell potential for the Zn/Cu galvanic cell as a function of log Q at 25°C.

questions.stem: What is E°cell for the Zn/Cu cell?

explanations.D: Correct. E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10 V. The positive voltage indicates a spontaneous galvanic reaction under standard conditions.
explanations.A: The sign is positive for the spontaneous cell as written.
explanations.B: 0.42 V comes from adding the potentials incorrectly without accounting for the anode sign.
explanations.C: 0.34 V is only the copper half-cell reduction potential, not the full cell voltage.

questions.stem: For the nonstandard trial with Q = 0.010, how does the voltage compare with 1.10 V?

choices.D: It must equal 1.10 V because solids are present.

explanations.A: Correct. Nernst gives E = E° - (0.0592/2)logQ. When Q = 0.010, logQ = -2, so the subtraction of a negative term raises E to about 1.16 V. Low product concentration and high reactant concentration favor the forward reaction.
explanations.B: A lower voltage would occur for Q > 1, not Q < 1.
explanations.C: The cell still has a favorable reaction and does not have zero voltage.
explanations.D: Solids are omitted from Q, but aqueous ion concentrations still change the voltage.

questions.stem: Which change would decrease the cell voltage?

explanations.C: Correct. Increasing [Zn2+] increases Q for the reaction, making the reaction less product-favored and lowering E according to the Nernst equation. This is visible in the figure because voltage declines as logQ increases.
explanations.A: Decreasing [Zn2+] lowers Q and would raise cell voltage.
explanations.B: Increasing [Cu2+] lowers Q because Cu2+ is in the denominator, which would raise E.
explanations.D: Solid copper is not included in Q; changing the written expression that way is chemically incorrect.

questions.stem: At equilibrium, what is true of the cell voltage?

choices.C: Ecell remains 1.10 V for all concentrations
choices.D: Electrons stop existing in the circuit

explanations.B: Correct. At equilibrium there is no net driving force for the redox reaction, so the cell potential is zero. The reaction quotient has reached the equilibrium constant, linking electrochemistry and thermodynamics through ΔG = -nFE.
explanations.A: Q is not zero at equilibrium; it equals Keq.
explanations.C: The standard voltage applies only under standard-state conditions, not all concentrations.
explanations.D: Electrons still exist; there is simply no net spontaneous electron flow.

questions.passage: In an electrolysis experiment, a current was passed through AgNO3(aq) to plate Ag(s) onto a metal spoon. The cathode reaction was Ag+(aq) + e- -> Ag(s). In Trial 1, a 2.00 A current ran for 30.0 minutes. Faraday's constant is 96,485 C/mol e-, and the molar mass of Ag is 107.9 g/mol. A second trial used CuSO4(aq), in which Cu2+(aq) + 2e- -> Cu(s), to compare how electron stoichiometry affects plated mass.
questions.stem: In the AgNO3 electrolysis trial, where does silver metal plate?

explanations.A: Correct. Reduction occurs at the cathode in both galvanic and electrolytic cells. Since Ag+ gains an electron to become Ag(s), silver plates onto the cathode surface.
explanations.B: The anode is where oxidation occurs, not Ag+ reduction.
explanations.C: A salt bridge maintains charge balance in galvanic cells but is not where plating occurs here.
explanations.D: The power supply drives the reaction but is not the electrode surface receiving Ag(s).

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Predicted plated mass for Ag and Cu under the same current and time conditions.

questions.stem: How many coulombs pass in Trial 1?

explanations.D: Correct. Charge equals current multiplied by time. A 2.00 A current for 30.0 min is 2.00 C/s × 1800 s = 3600 C.
explanations.A: 60 C ignores the conversion from minutes to seconds and the current magnitude.
explanations.B: 120 C would correspond to 2.00 A for only 60 seconds.
explanations.C: 96,485 C is one Faraday, not the charge passed in this trial.

questions.stem: Approximately how much Ag is plated in Trial 1?

explanations.C: Correct. Moles of electrons = 3600/96485 = 0.0373 mol. Because Ag+ requires one electron per Ag atom, 0.0373 mol Ag plates. Multiplying by 107.9 g/mol gives about 4.03 g of silver.
explanations.A: 0.037 is the approximate number of moles, not grams.
explanations.B: 2.02 g would incorrectly divide by 2 even though Ag+ uses one electron.
explanations.D: 8.06 g would double the correct mass without a stoichiometric basis.

questions.stem: Why is less Cu mass plated than Ag under the same current and time?

choices.B: Cu2+ requires two electrons per atom and copper has a lower molar mass.
choices.A: Cu2+ is reduced at the anode.
choices.D: Ag+ requires two electrons per atom while Cu2+ requires one.

explanations.B: Correct. The same charge supplies the same moles of electrons. Copper deposition consumes two electrons per Cu atom, and Cu also has a lower molar mass than Ag, so the plated copper mass is smaller under identical electrical conditions.
explanations.A: Cu2+ is reduced at the cathode, not the anode.
explanations.C: Current determines charge delivered over time and directly affects plated amount.
explanations.D: The electron stoichiometry is reversed; Ag+ uses one electron and Cu2+ uses two.

questions.stem: If the current in the silver trial were doubled while time stayed constant, what would happen to the mass of Ag plated?

choices.A: It would be cut in half.
choices.C: It would become independent of Faraday's constant.

explanations.D: Correct. Plated mass is proportional to charge, and charge is current × time. Doubling current doubles the delivered charge, doubles moles of electrons, and therefore doubles moles and mass of Ag deposited.
explanations.A: Halving would occur if current or time were halved, not doubled.
explanations.B: Mass cannot remain unchanged when twice as much charge passes through the cell.
explanations.C: Faraday's constant still converts charge to moles of electrons.
